verificationNote: Paraphrased from IEC 62443. Obtain normative text from iec.ch.
description: Security Levels define the degree of protection required for a zone or conduit against a defined threat category. Three variants exist: SL-T (Target — what you need), SL-A (Achieved — what you have), SL-C (Capability — what a product can provide). Asset owners set SL-T per zone during risk assessment; implementation must achieve SL-A >= SL-T.

levels.label: Basic
levels.shortDescription: Protection against unintentional or casual violation
levels.threatProfile: Casual or coincidental attacker — opportunistic malware, inadvertent user error, generic ransomware without OT-specific capability

exampleActors: Disgruntled employee with basic IT access
Contractor with legitimate but misused access

typicalApplicability: Environmental sensors with no process control
Office-adjacent OT systems with no safety impact
levels.malaysiaContext: Minimum baseline for any OT environment. Non-NCII-designated OT operators in Malaysia should achieve at least SL 1.
levels.controlCharacteristics: Password authentication, basic network separation, documented procedures, standard AV where applicable

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.shortDescription: Protection against intentional violation using simple means with low resources and generic skills
levels.threatProfile: Motivated attacker with generic skills — targeted cybercriminal, hacktivist, or insider with operational intent but limited OT expertise

attackerCharacteristics.motivation: Intentional — financial gain, disruption, reputational damage
attackerCharacteristics.resources: Low to medium — commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) attack tools
attackerCharacteristics.knowledge: General IT attack skills; some awareness of OT/SCADA but no deep protocol expertise
exampleActors: Targeted ransomware group (e.g., Cl0p, LockBit pivoting from IT to OT)
Disgruntled insider with operational IT access

levels.malaysiaContext: Baseline for NCII-designated OT operators under Act 854. s18 security measures, s21 risk assessment, and s23 audit requirements are substantially met at SL 2 for most sectors. Recommended minimum for all NACSA-designated NCII entities with OT environments.
levels.controlCharacteristics: MFA, role-based access control, network segmentation, patch management programme, security monitoring, incident response plan, supply chain security baseline

levels.label: Advanced
levels.shortDescription: Protection against intentional violation using sophisticated means with moderate resources and IACS-specific skills
levels.threatProfile: Skilled attacker with OT expertise — advanced criminal group or early-stage state-sponsored actor with IACS-specific capability

attackerCharacteristics.motivation: Intentional — disruption of critical services, data exfiltration, intelligence gathering
attackerCharacteristics.resources: Medium to high — custom tools, dedicated OT research capability
attackerCharacteristics.knowledge: Deep OT protocol knowledge (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP), multi-stage attack planning
exampleActors: Advanced cybercriminal groups with OT capability

typicalApplicability: Major water distribution for large population centres
levels.malaysiaContext: Required for Tier 1 NCII-designated entities — primary power generation and transmission assets (TNB, Petronas majors), national water infrastructure (Air Selangor, SAJ), major port/airport control systems. NACSA sector leads should mandate SL 3 for their highest-criticality NCII assets.
levels.controlCharacteristics: Hardware-based MFA, deep packet inspection for OT protocols, security operations centre (SOC) with OT visibility, red team exercises, unidirectional gateways for highest-risk conduits, vendor access management, security by design in procurement

levels.label: Critical
levels.shortDescription: Protection against intentional violation using state-of-the-art means with extended resources and exceptional IACS skills
levels.threatProfile: Nation-state actor with unlimited resources, zero-day capability, and demonstrated ability to target safety systems

attackerCharacteristics.motivation: Strategic national interest — mass casualty, critical service collapse, geopolitical leverage
attackerCharacteristics.resources: Essentially unlimited — years of investment, dedicated OT research teams
attackerCharacteristics.knowledge: Full OT stack expertise including safety system internals, hardware implants, custom firmware modification
exampleActors: TRITON/TRISIS operators (targeted Schneider Triconex SIS at Saudi Aramco)
Stuxnet operators (targeted Siemens S7 centrifuge PLCs)
Sandworm (GRU Unit 74455 — Ukrainian power grid, NotPetya)

typicalApplicability: Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) at any criticality level
Strategic oil and gas safety systems
levels.malaysiaContext: Applicable to safety systems regardless of sector. Any Malaysian industrial operator running a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) — Triconex, Hima, Yokogawa ProSafe, ABB High Integrity — should target SL 4 for those systems regardless of their broader NCII designation status, given that SIS compromise can cause mass casualty events.
levels.controlCharacteristics: Air-gapped safety systems, hardware security modules, integrity-verified firmware, continuous safety system monitoring, independent safety network (not connected to DCS or historian), physical tamper detection, formal security review of all maintenance activities

slTargetingProcess.description: Zone-by-zone SL targeting is performed during the security risk assessment (IEC 62443-3-2). The process involves:

steps.action: Define zone boundaries based on function, criticality and consequence of compromise
Identify threat actors relevant to the sector and geopolitical context
Assess consequence of successful attack per zone (safety, operational, financial, reputational, environmental)
Set SL-T per zone — typically: SL 2 for operational zones, SL 3 for critical operational zones, SL 4 for safety systems
Assess current SL-A (achieved) per zone by measuring against SR requirements
Gap = SL-T minus SL-A — drives the remediation roadmap
